---
pagetitle: "The Silicon Sample Benchmark — Predicting a megastudy before the results are revealed"
description: "A multi-team benchmark of AI approaches for predicting the results of a large-scale behavioral megastudy (N ≈ 18,000) on trust in climate scientists. Method registration, submission specifications, and scoring."
format:
  html:
    page-layout: custom
    toc: false
    code-tools: false
    css: tournament.css
---

```{=html}
<div class="sst">

<header class="hero" id="top">
  <div class="inner">
    <p class="eyebrow">Call for participation · Multi-team prediction benchmark</p>
    <h1>Can your silicon sample predict how <em>18,000 humans</em> will behave?</h1>
    <p class="dek">We are fielding one of the largest behavioral megastudies to date — 16 message interventions to increase trust in climate scientists, 13 preregistered outcomes, a representative U.S. sample. The outcome data are locked and unpublished. Before they are released, we invite selected research teams to predict the results with any AI-based approach they choose: behavioral clones, group-level simulation, or direct effect forecasting. Every approach is registered, every prediction is locked, and every team is compared against the same human–human baseline. No single winner is crowned: the goal is a systematic, side-by-side overview of how different approaches perform across many metrics.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="hero-links" aria-label="Take part">
    <a class="hero-link" href="https://forms.gle/Z8j1goLtPKQmESSr8" target="_blank" rel="noopener">
      <span class="hl-no">1</span>
      <span class="hl-txt"><strong>Tell us you'd like to participate</strong><span class="hl-sub">Leave your contact details <span class="hl-due">· by Jul 24</span></span></span>
      <span class="hl-arrow" aria-hidden="true">→</span>
    </a>
    <a class="hero-link" href="preregistration_benchmark.html">
      <span class="hl-no">2</span>
      <span class="hl-txt"><strong>Read the preregistration</strong><span class="hl-sub">The benchmark preregistration</span></span>
      <span class="hl-arrow" aria-hidden="true">→</span>
    </a>
    <a class="hero-link" href="https://github.com/janpfander/silicon-sample-submission" target="_blank" rel="noopener">
      <span class="hl-no">3</span>
      <span class="hl-txt"><strong>Make your registration</strong><span class="hl-sub">Submission template repository <span class="hl-due">· lock by Aug 30</span></span></span>
      <span class="hl-arrow" aria-hidden="true">→</span>
    </a>
  </div>
  <div class="facts" aria-label="Key facts">
    <div class="cell"><div class="num">≈18,000</div><div class="lbl">Human participants, U.S. field experiment</div></div>
    <div class="cell"><div class="num">16</div><div class="lbl">Text-based interventions, never tested before</div></div>
    <div class="cell"><div class="num">13</div><div class="lbl">Preregistered outcomes, incl. behavioral measures</div></div>
    <div class="cell"><div class="num">6</div><div class="lbl">Demographic moderators for subgroup effects</div></div>
    <div class="cell"><div class="num">0</div><div class="lbl">Bits of human outcome data visible to any team</div></div>
  </div>
</header>

<div class="sst-shell">

<nav class="sst-toc" aria-label="On this page">
  <p class="toc-title">On this page</p>
  <ol id="sst-toc-list">
    <li><a href="#why">Why participate</a></li>
    <li><a href="#rules">Rules</a></li>
    <li><a href="#registration">Registration</a></li>
    <li><a href="#scoring">Evaluation</a></li>
    <li><a href="#steps">Steps</a></li>
    <li><a href="#contact">Questions &amp; contact</a></li>
  </ol>
</nav>

<main>

<!-- ============================== WHY ============================== -->
<section id="why">
  <div class="sec-head"><span class="no">01</span><h2>Why participate</h2></div>
  <p>Whether large language models can stand in for human participants in experiments is one of the most consequential open questions in behavioral science. The evidence so far is mixed and hard to interpret: most existing evaluations target human studies that were already published — and plausibly in the models' training data — and very few studies are credibly preregistered, so reported successes are entangled with researcher degrees of freedom in model and prompt choice.</p>
  <p>This benchmark study addresses these problems. The target is a genuinely novel megastudy: none of the 16 interventions has ever been tested in this specific form on the specific outcomes. The human data are held exclusively by the core team, and every prediction must be deposited and time-stamped <strong>before</strong> any human data are released.</p>
  <p>What you get out of it:</p>
  <ul class="plain">
    <li><strong>A credible test of your approach.</strong> Your method is evaluated on truly out-of-sample human behavior, against a preregistered human–human comparison.</li>
    <li><strong>Co-authorship on the benchmark paper.</strong> All participating teams join the resulting publication, which reports the full results table and a structured catalogue of approaches.</li>
    <li><strong>A catalogue of the field.</strong> Each registered method becomes a structured, comparable entry describing how teams currently approach AI prediction of behavioral experiments — open- vs. closed-weight models, persona sources, administration protocols, fine-tuning, in-context learning — and which of these choices appear to matter.</li>
    <li><strong>No single winner.</strong> Approaches are scored on a ladder of metrics of increasing strictness — from directional agreement to formal equivalence — plus distributional fidelity and stereotyping diagnostics. We expect different approaches to do well on different rungs; the point is to map the design space and compare approaches across metrics, not to crown one champion.</li>
  </ul>
  <div class="sst-callout">
    <span class="tag">The target study</span>
    <p>A between-subjects field experiment testing 16 text-based interventions to increase trust in climate scientists against a control condition. The sample is <strong>N ≈ 18,000</strong> U.S. adults (1,000 per intervention plus 2,000 control), recruited from an opt-in panel under <strong>census-based cross quotas</strong> on gender × age and gender × race/ethnicity. The primary outcome is a 12-item multidimensional trust composite; secondary and tertiary outcomes span funding perceptions, policy attitudes, institutional trust, climate beliefs and behaviors, and two behavioral measures: a real-money donation decision and a newsletter sign-up. See the full instrument on the <a href="questionnaire.html">questionnaire page</a> and the sample and quota detail in the <a href="preregistration_benchmark.html#the-human-target-sample">benchmark preregistration</a>.</p>
  </div>
</section>

<!-- ============================== RULES ============================== -->
<section id="rules">
  <div class="sec-head"><span class="no">02</span><h2>Rules</h2></div>
  <p>Any AI-based approach is welcome — any models, personas, prompts, fine-tuning, or prior literature — under a few firm ground rules.</p>
  <ul class="plain">
    <li><strong>Blinding is absolute.</strong> No team may access, solicit, or be shown any human outcome data from this study before the prediction lock. A signed attestation is part of registration (item I.3 of the form).</li>
    <li><strong>AI-based and automated.</strong> Pipelines must be AI-based and run without humans in the loop at prediction time. Human effort goes into <em>designing</em> the pipeline, not into adjusting its outputs.</li>
    <li><strong>Otherwise unrestricted.</strong> Any models (open or closed weight), any prompting or persona strategy, any external training data, prior studies, or meta-analytic knowledge may be used — as long as it is declared in the registration form.</li>
    <li><strong>Invited expert teams of up to two.</strong> Participation is by invitation, open to teams with a published track record in silicon-sample research (assessed through a short separate survey when you register your interest — not part of the registration itself). A team is at most two people; an individual may join any number of teams. We encourage invited teams to forward the call to other researchers they consider relevant.</li>
    <li><strong>Multiple submissions are encouraged — up to two per tier.</strong> Enter more than once with genuinely different approaches (say, a per-respondent simulation and a direct forecast); each is a separate registered entry, capped at two per tier.</li>
    <li><strong>Everything is registered — not everything must be public.</strong> The method form, the prediction files, and — strongly encouraged — code and raw model output logs are deposited on <a href="https://zenodo.org/">Zenodo</a> (a free research archive run by CERN that gives each deposit a permanent DOI and a time-stamp — done through a release on a linked GitHub repository) before the lock date. Teams with proprietary methods may seal parts of their registration under the <a href="#disclosure">disclosure policy</a>: locked and verifiable is mandatory, public is not.</li>
  </ul>

  <p>We deliberately welcome very different approaches. Submissions enter at one of <strong>three tiers</strong>, defined by the granularity of the prediction. The common currency across tiers is the set of <strong>preregistered estimands</strong> — the same quantities the human analysis produces — so every approach is scored on the rungs it can reach. <strong>Tier 1 is the preferred option:</strong> individual-level data flows through every analysis unchanged, so one Tier-1 entry is automatically scored on all of them — there is no need to also submit the same method at a lower tier. Otherwise, choose the highest tier your approach supports; lower tiers are simply eligible for fewer analyses.</p>

  <div class="tiers">
    <div class="tier">
      <span class="t-code">Tier 1 · Full</span>
      <h4>Individual-level synthetic data</h4>
      <p>A respondent-per-row dataset of behavioral clones: each synthetic participant completes the experiment in one condition. Flows through the preregistered analysis pipeline completely unchanged.</p>
      <p><strong>Eligible for everything:</strong> ATE recovery, calibration regression, response distributions, subgroup heterogeneity, demographic baselines, and stereotyping diagnostics.</p>
      <div class="grain">Grain: 1 row = 1 synthetic respondent</div>
    </div>
    <div class="tier">
      <span class="t-code">Tier 2 · Cell-level</span>
      <h4>Group-level predictions</h4>
      <p>A predicted mean per cell — per condition × outcome, and per condition × moderator-level × outcome — with a 95% <em>uncertainty interval on the cell mean itself</em> (the interval you believe has a 95% chance of containing the human cell mean; <strong>not</strong> the spread of individual responses). For approaches that simulate or reason about groups rather than individuals.</p>
      <p><strong>Eligible for:</strong> ATEs, calibration, subgroup heterogeneity, and demographic baselines. Not eligible for distribution-shape metrics (OVL, KS) or predictability regressions.</p>
      <div class="grain">Grain: 1 row = 1 cell (condition × [moderator level ×] outcome)</div>
    </div>
    <div class="tier">
      <span class="t-code">Tier 3 · Effect-level</span>
      <h4>Direct effect predictions</h4>
      <p>One predicted ATE per intervention × outcome, in original outcome units, with a 95% uncertainty interval on the ATE expressing the team's epistemic uncertainty. For zero-shot forecasting, in-context learning over the literature, meta-analytic priors, or hybrid pipelines.</p>
      <p><strong>Eligible for:</strong> directional agreement, Spearman ρ, Pearson r, RMSE, and calibration regression. Inferential agreement and TOST are computed from the submitted intervals.</p>
      <div class="grain">Grain: 1 row = 1 intervention × outcome estimate</div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <p class="small">There is <strong>no fixed or required number of synthetic respondents per condition</strong>, and pool size never enters scoring. A larger pool only shrinks the Monte-Carlo noise in your <em>own</em> cell estimates, with diminishing returns once the per-cell mean is stable — so simulate enough that your predicted means are not sampling artifacts, but there is no benefit to matching the human N. For reference, each submission is scored against <strong>one half of the human sample</strong> (≈500 per intervention, ≈1,000 in control).</p>

  <div class="sst-callout">
    <span class="tag">Multiple entries welcome</span>
    <p>We actively encourage teams to enter more than once — but only for <strong>genuinely different approaches</strong> (say, a per-respondent simulation and a direct forecast), not to restack one method across tiers. Each approach is a separate entry with its own repository and registration; the team designates one as its <strong>primary entry</strong> and the rest as <strong>secondary entries</strong>. This label is purely the team's own ranking of which result to treat as headline: <strong>every entry — primary or secondary — is scored identically, on the same pipeline, and reported in the results table.</strong> Comparing a team's simulated-then-derived effects against its own direct forecasts isolates the value of the simulation step itself — one of the most interesting questions this benchmark can answer.</p>
  </div>

  <h3>Analysis eligibility by tier</h3>
  <div class="tbl-wrap">
  <table>
    <thead><tr><th>Preregistered analysis</th><th>Tier 1</th><th>Tier 2</th><th>Tier 3</th></tr></thead>
    <tbody>
      <tr><td>ATE recovery — six-metric ladder (Section 1)<br><span class="small">Does the approach recover each intervention's average effect?</span></td><td class="ok">✓</td><td class="ok">✓</td><td class="ok">✓ *</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Calibration regression α / β (Section 1)<br><span class="small">Are predicted effects systematically biased or exaggerated?</span></td><td class="ok">✓</td><td class="ok">✓</td><td class="ok">✓</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Response distributions — variance ratio, OVL, KS (Section 1)<br><span class="small">Are the shapes of responses reproduced, not just means?</span></td><td class="ok">✓</td><td class="no-x">—</td><td class="no-x">—</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Subgroup heterogeneity — condition × moderator (Section 2)<br><span class="small">Do treatment effects vary across demographic groups as in humans?</span></td><td class="ok">✓</td><td class="ok">✓</td><td class="no-x">—</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Within-subgroup distributions (Section 2)<br><span class="small">Are response shapes reproduced inside each demographic group?</span></td><td class="ok">✓</td><td class="no-x">—</td><td class="no-x">—</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Demographic baseline calibration (Section 3)<br><span class="small">Do subgroup means in the control condition match humans?</span></td><td class="ok">✓</td><td class="ok">✓</td><td class="no-x">—</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Demographic predictability / stereotyping (Section 3)<br><span class="small">Do demographics predict responses with human-like strength, or do clones over-rely on them?</span></td><td class="ok">✓</td><td class="no-x">—</td><td class="no-x">—</td></tr>
    </tbody>
  </table>
  </div>
  <p class="small">* For Tiers 2 and 3, the submitted 95% uncertainty intervals are scored directly — coverage (how often the human value falls inside) and an interval score that rewards honest, narrow intervals — and additionally supply the standard errors for inferential agreement and equivalence (TOST). For Tier 1, standard errors fall out of the fitted models, so no separate uncertainty input is required. Full metric definitions are in the <a href="#scoring">Evaluation section</a> and the <a href="preregistration_benchmark.html#preregistered-analysis-plan">benchmark preregistration</a>.</p>
</section>

<!-- ============================== REGISTRATION ============================== -->
<section id="registration">
  <div class="sec-head"><span class="no">03</span><h2>Registration</h2></div>
  <p>There are <strong>two separate steps</strong>, easy to confuse:</p>
  <ul class="plain">
    <li><strong>Express interest — everyone, first.</strong> Every team fills the short <a href="https://forms.gle/Z8j1goLtPKQmESSr8" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Google interest form</a> to leave contact details and confirm participation. This is <em>not</em> where you pick a tier or submit anything — it just puts you on the list and opens the eligibility check. Even invited teams and colleagues you forward the call to must complete it.</li>
    <li><strong>Register your method — later, on Zenodo.</strong> The registration proper is the time-stamped <a href="https://zenodo.org/">Zenodo</a> deposit described below, produced from the <a href="https://github.com/janpfander/silicon-sample-submission" target="_blank" rel="noopener">submission template repository</a>. This is where the tier, the method description, and the predictions are locked.</li>
  </ul>
  <p>Before the lock, every team deposits one Zenodo record with two parts: a <strong>registration form</strong> describing the approach, and the <strong>prediction files</strong> it produced.</p>
  <p><strong>The form.</strong> A structured form documents the pipeline in enough detail that, in principle, an independent team could re-run it and obtain a statistically equivalent submission; where exact replication is impossible (deprecated APIs, hardware nondeterminism), the deposited raw output logs become the verifiable record. It builds on the <strong>GUIDE-LLM</strong> reporting checklist<sup class="fn"><span class="fn-ref">1</span><span class="fn-pop">Feuerriegel, S., Barrie, C., Crockett, M.&nbsp;J., et&nbsp;al. (2026). A reporting checklist for large language models in behavioural science. <em>Nature Human Behaviour</em>, 1–5. <a href="https://doi.org/10.1038/s41562-026-02492-7">https://doi.org/10.1038/s41562-026-02492-7</a></span></sup> — the consensus reporting standard for LLM use in behavioral science — extended where a checklist alone is not enough for full pipeline replicability: exact models and call dates, prompts and survey-administration protocol, persona/profile construction, stochasticity and aggregation, post-processing, any learning or retrieval corpora, internal model and prompt selection, and frozen code plus raw output logs. The full item-by-item form is below; a fillable template (Markdown + machine-readable YAML) ships in the <a href="materials.html">submission template</a> repository.</p>
  <p><strong>The predictions.</strong> Alongside the form you deposit the prediction file(s) for your <a href="#rules">tier</a>, a machine-readable <code>metadata.json</code>, and — strongly encouraged — code and raw model output logs. Everything is hashed and time-stamped before the lock; the DOIs and SHA-256 hashes are emailed to the core team by the same deadline. Column schemas, file naming, the <code>metadata.json</code> format, and a self-check validator are on the <a href="materials.html">materials page</a>.</p>
  <p><strong>The deposited, hashed record is final.</strong> Once you have emailed the DOI and fingerprints, that snapshot is your locked submission — it cannot be revised, and only the exact file matching the emailed SHA-256 hash is scored. You may keep working in your repository and even publish a newer Zenodo version afterwards, but the core team scores the version whose fingerprint you sent by the deadline. Deposit only when you are ready to lock.</p>

  <h3 id="registration-form">The registration form</h3>
  <p>The form builds on the <strong>GUIDE-LLM</strong> reporting checklist and extends it where a reporting checklist alone is not sufficient for, in principle, full replicability of a prediction pipeline. Items concerning human participants or sensitive-data privacy (GUIDE-LLM D.1) are generally not applicable here, since every submission is fully synthetic; mark these <code>N/A</code>. When multiple models serve different pipeline stages, complete the model sections separately per model.</p>

  <div class="legend">Item codes follow the GUIDE-LLM checklist, extended where needed for full pipeline replicability. <span class="star">★</span> marks items that must always be public — never escrowed or withheld; <span class="escrow">†</span> marks items that must be at minimum escrowed — they may be sealed from the public, but never withheld from the core team (see the <a href="#disclosure">disclosure policy</a>). Together the ★/† items form the non-waivable core that protects the validity of the benchmark. All other items may be escrowed freely, or withheld at the cost of having your entry classified as a sealed (Class C) entry.</div>

  <div class="tbl-wrap">
  <table>
    <caption>Registration form — part 1 · Identity, scope, and system</caption>
    <thead><tr><th style="width:120px">Item</th><th style="width:230px">Name</th><th>What to report</th></tr></thead>
    <tbody>
      <tr class="grp"><td colspan="3">0 · Approach identity and output</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">0.1 <span class="star">★</span></td><td>Team</td><td>Team name, the one or two members (teams are at most two people), affiliations, and a corresponding contact.</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">0.2 <span class="star">★</span></td><td>Plain-language summary</td><td>One paragraph describing the approach so that a non-specialist reader of the catalogue understands what was done.</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">0.3 <span class="star">★</span></td><td>Submission tier &amp; approach family</td><td>Tier (1 / 2 / 3) and approach family — e.g., one call per respondent vs. agent walking the full survey; single model vs. ensemble vs. multi-agent; simulation vs. direct forecasting; zero-shot vs. literature-conditioned.</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">0.4</td><td>Pipeline diagram</td><td>The full pipeline as an ordered sequence of steps (input → transformation → output), covering every stage from raw inputs to the submitted file. Prose descriptions of multi-stage pipelines are where replicability dies; a numbered step list or diagram is required.</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">0.5 <span class="star">★</span></td><td>Coverage</td><td>Number of synthetic respondents / cells / estimates; mapping to conditions; coverage of the 16 interventions and 13 outcomes (all, or a declared subset with justification).</td></tr>

      <tr class="grp"><td colspan="3">A · Scope of LLM use <span class="small">(GUIDE-LLM)</span></td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">A.1</td><td>Purpose</td><td>Every workflow stage where LLMs are used — at minimum generating predictions; also persona construction, prompt optimization, post-processing, literature digestion, etc.</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">A.2 <span class="star">★</span></td><td>Degree of automation</td><td>Confirmation that the pipeline is fully automated with no human in the loop at prediction time (a benchmark requirement); any exception and the oversight provided.</td></tr>

      <tr class="grp"><td colspan="3">B · Model / system details <span class="small">(GUIDE-LLM, extended)</span></td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">B.1</td><td>Model name(s)</td><td>Exact identifiers including provider, size, version/timestamp, and source link — e.g., <code>gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18</code> (OpenAI), <code>Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct</code> (Meta, via HuggingFace). If several models were tested, name all and state which produced the submission and why (see also J.1).</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">B.2</td><td>Access &amp; context mode</td><td>Access route (API / web / local), API name and version, chat vs. stateless calls, and <strong>exact call dates</strong> — providers update hosted models silently, so the date window is part of the model identity.</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">B.3</td><td>Configuration</td><td>All settings affecting output: temperature, top-p / top-k, max tokens, penalties, stop sequences, seeds, reasoning-effort settings, and number of completions per respondent / item.</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">B.4</td><td>Customization</td><td>Any adaptation beyond standard inference: fine-tuning (method, e.g., LoRA), RAG, automated prompt optimization, tool use, web search, agentic scaffolds, post-training. Cross-reference Section H for the data behind each.</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">B.5</td><td>Persistent memory</td><td>Whether sessions carried memory across interactions (yes / no / N/A), and if yes, what persisted.</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">B.6</td><td>Inference stack</td><td>For locally run models: serving framework and version (e.g., vLLM, llama.cpp, transformers), quantization (FP16 / INT8 / GGUF Q4 …), and hardware where nondeterminism matters. These change outputs and are invisible to GUIDE-LLM B.1–B.3 alone.</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">B.7</td><td>Ensembles</td><td>If multiple models or runs are combined: all members and the exact aggregation rule (averaging, voting, stacking, router logic).</td></tr>
    </tbody>
  </table>
  </div>

  <div class="tbl-wrap">
  <table>
    <caption>Registration form — part 2 · Conditioning, administration, and stochasticity</caption>
    <thead><tr><th style="width:120px">Item</th><th style="width:230px">Name</th><th>What to report</th></tr></thead>
    <tbody>
      <tr class="grp"><td colspan="3">C · Prompts <span class="small">(GUIDE-LLM)</span></td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">C.1</td><td>Exact prompts</td><td>Verbatim prompt text(s) including any in-context examples, or a link to them in the deposited repository. State whether prompts were iteratively refined, and whether refinements were pre-specified or made in response to interim outputs.</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">C.2</td><td>System-wide instructions</td><td>Any system-level instructions guiding the model's general behavior.</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">C.3</td><td>Prompt-design rationale</td><td>A brief rationale for the prompt design: why prompts were structured as they were, and the reasoning behind any major design choices. Recommended, not required.</td></tr>

      <tr class="grp"><td colspan="3">D · Persona / profile construction <span class="small">(Tiers 1–2)</span></td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">D.1</td><td>Profile source</td><td>Source of demographic profiles you constructed: a public survey (e.g. GSS / ANES / Census), other survey, fully synthetic, or none. The benchmark ships no participant pool; report how you built yours, including how profiles were assigned to conditions.</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">D.2</td><td>Profile verbalization</td><td>Which profile variables are encoded and exactly how they are rendered into text — fixed template vs. LLM-generated persona narratives (if the latter: the generating model and prompt, recursively subject to this form).</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">D.3</td><td>Assignment &amp; weighting</td><td>Number of personas, assignment to conditions (between- vs. within-subjects)—the team's responsibility, covering all 17 conditions—reuse across conditions, and any weighting or matching to a target population.</td></tr>

      <tr class="grp"><td colspan="3">E · Stimulus and survey administration</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">E.1</td><td>Stimulus presentation</td><td>How interventions are shown to the model: verbatim text vs. paraphrase or summary; how state-contingent content (e.g., the extreme-weather intervention) is handled.</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">E.2</td><td>Survey walk-through</td><td>How items are administered: one item per call vs. blocks vs. whole survey; whether context carries across items; item and response-option ordering and any randomization; how response scales are displayed; how attention/comprehension elements are handled. The goal: enough detail to re-run the survey walk deterministically.</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">E.3</td><td>Response elicitation</td><td>How responses are obtained: free text, constrained choice, structured output, or token log-probabilities over the response scale (if logprobs: the normalization and mapping to scale values).</td></tr>

      <tr class="grp"><td colspan="3">F · Stochasticity and aggregation</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">F.1</td><td>Runs &amp; seeds</td><td>Number of runs per respondent / item / estimate; seeds where applicable; expected reproducibility of outputs under identical settings.</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">F.2</td><td>Aggregation rule</td><td>How multiple generations are consolidated into submitted values: mean, median, mode, first valid response, sampling one, distribution-preserving sampling. GUIDE-LLM asks for temperature; this item asks what you <em>did</em> with the variance — it directly shapes the distributional metrics.</td></tr>
    </tbody>
  </table>
  </div>

  <div class="tbl-wrap">
  <table>
    <caption>Registration form — part 3 · Post-processing, learning components, blinding, and selection</caption>
    <thead><tr><th style="width:120px">Item</th><th style="width:230px">Name</th><th>What to report</th></tr></thead>
    <tbody>
      <tr class="grp"><td colspan="3">G · Validation &amp; post-processing <span class="small">(GUIDE-LLM, extended)</span></td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">G.1</td><td>Human validation</td><td>Whether and how any human review of model outputs was performed (often N/A for fully automated generation; note any design-phase checks on pilot outputs).</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">G.2</td><td>Post-processing</td><td>Parsing rules from raw output to response scales; handling of refusals, malformed, missing, and out-of-range responses (re-prompt / drop / clip / impute); any exclusion criteria. For approaches that generate individual responses, also report the resulting effective N per condition (counts of valid generations behind each cell — this is descriptive disclosure, not a scoring input). Refusal handling alone can move distributions substantially — report counts.</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">G.3</td><td>Calibration corrections</td><td>Any post-hoc scaling, shifting, debiasing, or calibration applied to predictions — and exactly what data it was fit on (cross-reference H and I).</td></tr>

      <tr class="grp"><td colspan="3">H · Learning and conditioning components</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">H.1</td><td>Fine-tuning data</td><td>For any fine-tuned component: the exact training corpus (with hashes or DOIs), hyperparameters, and checkpoint identifiers.</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">H.2</td><td>Context &amp; retrieval corpora</td><td>For ICL / RAG / literature-conditioned approaches: the exact document set placed in context or indexed for retrieval, archived in the deposit. For direct-effect forecasters this corpus <em>is</em> the core of the method and must be fully enumerated.</td></tr>

      <tr class="grp"><td colspan="3">I · Data inputs, blinding, and competing interests <span class="small">(GUIDE-LLM, extended)</span></td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">I.1 <span class="star">★</span></td><td>Competing interests</td><td>Funding, in-kind compute or model access, and professional or financial relationships with entities that have an interest in LLMs — particularly relevant where teams evaluate models built by affiliated organizations.</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">I.2 <span class="escrow">†</span></td><td>External human data</td><td>A self-declared list of all external human datasets that informed the approach anywhere (training, fine-tuning, retrieval, in-context examples, calibration anchors) — particularly published trust-in-science or climate-communication experiments.</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">I.3 <span class="star">★</span></td><td>Blinding attestation</td><td><strong>Mandatory.</strong> A signed attestation that no team member accessed, solicited, or was shown any human outcome data from this study, including pilots, before the prediction lock.</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">I.4 <span class="escrow">†</span></td><td>Contamination note</td><td>Training cutoff of every model used, relative to the public release dates of this project's preregistrations and Zenodo deposits — which are themselves potential contamination vectors for late-cutoff models. Note any known exposure of models to the project's materials.</td></tr>

      <tr class="grp"><td colspan="3">J · Internal selection procedure</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">J.1 <span class="escrow">†</span></td><td>Design-space search</td><td>How the team arrived at the final pipeline: how many candidate configurations (models, prompts, hyperparameters) were tried; the internal validation criterion; and what data internal validation was run against. This is the researcher-degrees-of-freedom analog for simulation pipelines — invisible to GUIDE-LLM, but without it the benchmark partly measures luck in configuration lotteries rather than method quality.</td></tr>

      <tr class="grp"><td colspan="3">K · Reproducibility &amp; frozen artifacts <span class="small">(GUIDE-LLM F.1, extended)</span></td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">K.1</td><td>Code &amp; materials</td><td>Code, notebooks, prompts, and configuration shared (link / DOI), with secrets removed; determinism and seeds documented.</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">K.2 <span class="escrow">†</span></td><td>Raw output logs</td><td>Complete, unprocessed model responses archived alongside the processed submission, hashed and time-stamped in the Zenodo deposit. Code is necessary but insufficient: when a hosted model is deprecated, the logs are what remains verifiable. Required for Tiers 1–2 (public or escrowed — a withheld log makes the entry Class C); for Tier 3, required where intermediate generations exist. Logs too large for a GitHub release can be a separate Zenodo upload linked here.</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">K.3</td><td>Computational resources</td><td>API-call counts, total tokens, financial cost, and compute time — a GUIDE-LLM optional item made standard here, since cost-per-fidelity is itself a benchmark dimension.</td></tr>
    </tbody>
  </table>
  </div>

  <h3 id="disclosure">Disclosure policy — proprietary approaches</h3>
  <p>Some teams — companies especially — cannot or will not publicly disclose their full approach. The benchmark separates two things usually conflated: <strong>verifiability</strong> (is the registration locked, time-stamped, and auditable?) and <strong>publicity</strong> (can everyone read it?). Verifiability is non-negotiable; publicity is graded. Each item is deposited as <em>public</em> (in the open Zenodo record), <em>escrowed</em> (in a restricted-access Zenodo record that still carries a DOI, a timestamp, and public SHA-256 content hashes — so the lock is verifiable — but is readable only by the core team and up to two independent auditors under a confidentiality agreement; an embargo with a sunset date, e.g. release upon publication or after 24 months, is encouraged), or <em>withheld</em> (not deposited at all, permitted only for items marked neither <span class="star">★</span> nor <span class="escrow">†</span> in the <a href="#registration-form">registration form</a> above). An entry's <strong>disclosure class</strong> follows from its most restricted item:</p>
  <div class="tbl-wrap">
  <table>
    <thead><tr><th style="width:170px">Class</th><th style="width:280px">Definition</th><th>Consequences</th></tr></thead>
    <tbody>
      <tr><td class="item-code">Class A · Open</td><td>All registration items public.</td><td>Full results-table standing; entry is independently replicable in principle; all features enter the approach catalogue and the design-choice analysis.</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">Class B · Escrowed</td><td>Some items sealed, but every item is available to the core team and auditors under confidentiality.</td><td>Full results-table standing with an <em>escrowed</em> badge; compliance is verified but independent replication awaits the embargo sunset; only publicly disclosed features enter the design-choice analysis (the rest coded as undisclosed).</td></tr>
      <tr><td class="item-code">Class C · Sealed</td><td>One or more permitted items withheld even from escrow.</td><td>Scored and reported in the results table with a <em>sealed / not independently verifiable</em> flag; excluded from the approach catalogue and the design-choice analysis; the manuscript reports the entry as a performance claim that the core team could not audit beyond the always-public and escrowed-minimum items.</td></tr>
    </tbody>
  </table>
  </div>
  <div class="sst-callout">
    <span class="tag">How it works in practice</span>
    <p>Nothing is escrowed automatically. You set <strong>one disclosure choice per item</strong> — public, escrowed, or (only for items marked neither <span class="star">★</span> nor <span class="escrow">†</span>) withheld — as you fill in the registration form, and you deposit public items in the open Zenodo record and escrowed items in the restricted one. Your entry's overall <strong>disclosure class comes from your single most restricted item</strong> — you don't choose it separately. You write it into <code>metadata.json</code> as <code>disclosure_class</code> so it is machine-readable, but it has to match your item choices. So one withheld optional item makes the whole entry Class C; keep everything at least escrowed and it is Class B; make everything public and it is Class A.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="sst-callout warn">
    <span class="tag">Why participate at all, if you can't disclose?</span>
    <p>For commercial teams, the value runs the other way: this is an adversarially designed, preregistered, third-party benchmark on genuinely unseen human data — a substantially stronger credibility signal than any internal evaluation, available at whichever disclosure class your IP constraints allow. The results table reports performance regardless of class; the badge tells readers exactly how much of the claim was auditable.</p>
  </div>

</section>

<!-- ============================== SCORING ============================== -->
<section id="scoring">
  <div class="sec-head"><span class="no">04</span><h2>Evaluation</h2></div>
  <div class="sst-callout">
    <span class="tag">The human–human baseline</span>
    <p>Scoring follows the <a href="preregistration_benchmark.html#preregistered-analysis-plan">preregistered analysis plan</a> without modification. A preregistered random seed splits the human sample in half. Every submission is scored against <strong>Human 1</strong>. The agreement between <strong>Human 1 and Human 2</strong> is the human–human baseline: how closely two independent halves of the human sample agree on the same estimands. This is a natural reference point for interpreting every method's scores, and it is reported in the same row format as every team.</p>
  </div>

  <p>Every submission is scored against <strong>Human 1</strong> on every metric and comparison its <a href="#rules">tier</a> supports, across all 13 outcomes. The ATE-recovery ladder runs from least to most strict — knowing on which rung an approach breaks is more informative than any single pass/fail verdict. The scored effect for each outcome is a <strong>between-subjects post-treatment contrast</strong> — the intervention-group mean minus the control-group mean. There is no baseline adjustment; the survey's pre-treatment measures are not used in scoring.</p>
  <div class="tbl-wrap">
  <table>
    <caption>Scoring metrics and comparisons. The ATE-recovery ladder is ordered from least to most strict; the further comparisons are computed wherever the submission's tier supports them.</caption>
    <thead><tr><th style="width:260px">Metric / comparison</th><th>What it asks</th></tr></thead>
    <tbody>
      <tr class="grp"><td colspan="2">ATE-recovery ladder — increasing strictness, top to bottom</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Directional agreement</td><td>Do you get the sign of each effect right? Chance = 50%.</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Spearman ρ</td><td>Do you rank the interventions in the human order?</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Pearson r</td><td>Are your effects proportional to human effects?</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Inferential agreement</td><td>Would a researcher using your data reach the same significance conclusions?</td></tr>
      <tr><td>RMSE</td><td>How large is the absolute magnitude error, in outcome units?</td></tr>
      <tr><td>TOST equivalence</td><td>Is each predicted effect statistically equivalent to the human effect within ±50%?</td></tr>
      <tr class="grp"><td colspan="2">Further comparisons — wherever the tier supports it</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Calibration regression</td><td>Human ATEs regressed on predicted ATEs, separating additive bias (intercept α) from proportionality (slope β), with F-tests of β = 1 and of the joint α = 0, β = 1.</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Subgroup effects</td><td>Recovery of condition × moderator interaction effects — whether treatment effects vary across the six demographic moderators as they do in humans, scored on the same six-rung ladder.</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Distributional metrics <span class="small">(Tier 1)</span></td><td>Variance ratio, distribution overlap coefficient (OVL), and Kolmogorov–Smirnov distance — whether response <em>shapes</em> are reproduced, not just means; also within subgroups for the primary outcome.</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Demographic baseline calibration</td><td>Pearson r and RMSE of subgroup means against humans in the control condition, computed per moderator.</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Demographic predictability / stereotyping</td><td>R² from separate per-moderator regressions — whether demographics predict synthetic responses with the same strength as in humans, or whether clones over-rely on demographic cues.</td></tr>
    </tbody>
  </table>
  </div>
  <p>The exact estimands, metric definitions, and the full scoring pipeline — including how each metric above is computed and reported — are specified in the benchmark preregistration. It is the single authoritative source for how submissions are evaluated.</p>
  <div class="cta-row" style="margin-top:18px;display:flex;gap:14px;flex-wrap:wrap">
    <a class="btn btn-primary" href="preregistration_benchmark.html">Read the full scoring methodology &amp; analyses →</a>
  </div>
</section>

<!-- ============================== STEPS ============================== -->
<section id="steps">
  <div class="sec-head"><span class="no">05</span><h2>Steps</h2></div>
  <p>Participation is by invitation. Once you accept, you build your pipeline with any AI-based approach — any models, personas, prompts, fine-tuning, retrieval, or prior literature — using the intervention texts and the full survey instrument, and constructing your own participant profiles (the benchmark ships no pool), but never any human outcome data. You then register the approach and deposit your predictions before the lock. The roadmap marks which milestones are your team's responsibility (<span class="who you">Your task</span>) and which are the core team's (<span class="who core">Core team</span>).</p>

  <div class="timeline">
    <div class="tl-item">
      <span class="date">July 01, 2026 <span class="who core">Core team</span></span>
      <h4>Invitations &amp; materials</h4>
      <p>Invited teams receive the intervention texts, the survey instrument, the codebook, the registration template, and the validator — never a participant pool (teams build their own) and never any human outcome data.</p>
    </div>
    <div class="tl-item has-action">
      <div class="tl-body">
        <span class="date">July 24, 2026 <span class="who you">Your task</span></span>
        <h4>Confirm intent</h4>
        <p>Confirm participation by leaving your contact details in the interest form, then build your pipeline. Your tier is declared later, at registration.</p>
      </div>
      <a class="tl-action" href="https://forms.gle/Z8j1goLtPKQmESSr8" target="_blank" rel="noopener">
        <span class="tl-action-label">Participate</span>
        <span class="tl-action-sub">Leave your contact details →</span>
      </a>
    </div>
    <div class="tl-item">
      <span class="date">July 30, 2026 <span class="who core">Core team</span></span>
      <h4>Human data collection completes</h4>
      <p>The remaining human responses (toward N ≈ 18,000) are collected and cleaned, then held sealed — not shared, released, or analyzed until after the prediction lock.</p>
    </div>
    <div class="tl-item hard has-action">
      <div class="tl-body">
        <span class="date">August 30, 2026 · Hard deadline <span class="who you">Your task</span></span>
        <h4>Submission deadline — register &amp; lock</h4>
        <p>Complete the registration form, generate your predictions, and deposit the form, prediction files, and logs on Zenodo — public or partly escrowed under the <a href="#disclosure">disclosure policy</a>, hashed and time-stamped, with the hashes emailed by the same deadline. No revisions after.</p>
      </div>
      <a class="tl-action" href="https://github.com/janpfander/silicon-sample-submission" target="_blank" rel="noopener">
        <span class="tl-action-label">Register</span>
        <span class="tl-action-sub">Submission template repo →</span>
      </a>
    </div>
    <div class="tl-item">
      <span class="date">September 30, 2026 <span class="who core">Core team</span></span>
      <h4>Scoring &amp; first manuscript draft</h4>
      <p>Every submission runs through the locked analysis pipeline unchanged; the results table and catalogue are assembled, and a first draft is shared with all co-authoring teams.</p>
    </div>
    <div class="tl-item">
      <span class="date">October 31, 2026 <span class="who core">Joint</span></span>
      <h4>Pre-print &amp; submission</h4>
      <p>The joint manuscript is posted as a pre-print and submitted for publication, with all participating teams as co-authors.</p>
    </div>
  </div>
  <p class="small">The human study was already partially fielded (≈10,000 of ≈18,000 responses) when the benchmark preregistration was drafted; none of these data have been shared, published, or made accessible to any prospective team.</p>

</section>

<!-- ============================== CONTACT ============================== -->
<section id="contact">
  <div class="sec-head"><span class="no">06</span><h2>Questions &amp; contact</h2></div>
  <p>Participation is by invitation; if you believe your team should be on the list, or you have questions about the registration form, the schemas, or edge cases of the rules, head to the contact page.</p>
  <div class="cta-row" style="margin-top:8px;display:flex;gap:14px;flex-wrap:wrap">
    <a class="btn btn-primary" href="contact.html">Go to the contact page</a>
  </div>
</section>

</main>

</div>

<script>
(function () {
  var links = Array.prototype.slice.call(document.querySelectorAll('#sst-toc-list a'));
  if (!links.length) return;
  var byId = {};
  var targets = links.map(function (a) {
    var el = document.getElementById(a.getAttribute('href').slice(1));
    if (el) byId[el.id] = a;
    return el;
  }).filter(Boolean);
  var obs = new IntersectionObserver(function (entries) {
    entries.forEach(function (e) {
      if (e.isIntersecting) {
        links.forEach(function (l) { l.classList.remove('active'); });
        if (byId[e.target.id]) byId[e.target.id].classList.add('active');
      }
    });
  }, { rootMargin: '-15% 0px -75% 0px', threshold: 0 });
  targets.forEach(function (t) { obs.observe(t); });
})();
</script>

</div>
```
